issues.title: [web] Chrome on iOS does not hide Autofill suggestions
issues.body:
Given this configuration for a `TextField` :
```dart
TextField(
    autocorrect: false,
    enableSuggestions: false,
    autofillHints: null,
    keyboardType: TextInputType.multiline,   // TextInputType.text has the same behavior
    maxLines: null,
),
```

This is how it appears on a release build on iOS browsers:

Chrome             |         Safari 
:-------------------------:|:-------------------------:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/46427323/115117646-54258800-9fa8-11eb-9d53-4e40976def7a.png) |   ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/46427323/115117656-5a1b6900-9fa8-11eb-9ea3-c56f3b069162.png)

* for Safari, when not in private/incognito mode, it shows `autofill contact`. 

### Expected Behavior
I expected the autofill options to disappear for the given keyboard type and configuration (mainly the `keyboardType`).  


###  Pain Points:
- It takes unnecessary real estate. It becomes even worst when suggestions are enabled and  a richtext toolbar is added above it.
- It gives the wrong impression to the user when the field is not meant to be form field (e.g. messaging or social media post). 

I'm not sure what is this toolbar is called, but it seems like it's specific for form fields navigation based on the arrows and the done button. It'd be useful to either hide the entire toolbar based on the keyboardType or provide a flag to hide it. 

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Given the sample code below.
2. flutter build web --release 
3. Serve the build (e.g., `python3 -m http.server --directory build/web 8000`) 
4. Open the web app in a physical iPhone browser. (I guess the simulator browser would do too).

<details><summary>minimal sample code to reproduce</summary>

```dart
import 'package:flutter/material.dart';

void main() {
  runApp(MyApp());
}

class MyApp extends StatelessWidget {
  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return MaterialApp(
      title: 'Flutter Demo',
      theme: ThemeData(
        primarySwatch: Colors.blue,
      ),
      home: MyHomePage(title: 'Flutter Demo Home Page'),
    );
  }
}

class MyHomePage extends StatelessWidget {
  MyHomePage({Key key, this.title}) : super(key: key);

  final String title;

  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return Scaffold(
      appBar: AppBar(
        title: Text(title),
      ),
      body: Center(
        child: SingleChildScrollView(
          child: Column(
            mainAxisAlignment: MainAxisAlignment.center,
            children: <Widget>[
              TextField(
                autocorrect: false,
                enableSuggestions: false,
                autofillHints: null,
                // TextInputType.text has the same behavior
                keyboardType: TextInputType.multiline, 
                maxLines: null,
              ),
            ],
          ),
        ),
      ),
    );
  }
}

```

</details>

issues.title: TextField labelText doesn't get hintTextStyle when labelTextStyle is non-null
issues.body:
## Steps to Reproduce

<!-- You must include full steps to reproduce so that we can reproduce the problem. -->

1. You can have a look at this little reproduction [https://dartpad.dev/?id=b401619acd87ab339da95a0c8e216e79&null_safety=true](https://dartpad.dev/?id=b401619acd87ab339da95a0c8e216e79&null_safety=true)

**Expected results:** <!-- what did you want to see? -->
[The documentation for InputDecoration](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/material/InputDecoration/hintStyle.html) states that a TextField's `labelText` should get the `hintTextStyle` applied when the label is sitting on top of the input.
In the reproduction case, I expect the TextField label to be blue while TextField doesn't has focus or value.

**Actual results:**
`labelText` gets `labelTextStyle` values applied even while sitting on top of the input


I also had a quick look into TextField source, and found that in InputDecorator, `labelTextStyle` is merged into `inlineLabelStyle`, which explains why the `labelText` color always prevails. https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/f4abaa0735eba4dfd8f33f73363911d63931fe03/packages/flutter/lib/src/material/input_decorator.dart#L2209-L2211

What's moreover, the `inlineStyle` doesn't seem to be taking `hintTextStyle` into account at all
https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/f4abaa0735eba4dfd8f33f73363911d63931fe03/packages/flutter/lib/src/material/input_decorator.dart#L2106-L2109

issues.title: Neither Japanese, Korean nor Chinese keyboards are displayed for password text field (material and cupertino)
issues.body:
### Is there an existing issue for this?

- [X] I have searched the [existing issues](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues)
- [X] I have read the [guide to filing a bug](https://flutter.dev/docs/resources/bug-reports)

### Steps to reproduce

1. Add the keyboard Japanese (or Chinese or Korean) in your iPhone (14 Pro - iOS 16.6) or Android (Pixel 6 - Android 13)
2. Go to any app that includes the module text field like this demo: https://gallery.flutter.dev/#/demo/text-field 
3. Go to the password field
4. Try to select the Japanese (or Chinese or Korean) keyboard. They are not visible.

### Expected results

Password text field should support Japanese (Korean or Chinese) characters.

### Actual results

Neither Japanese, Korean nor Chinese keyboards are displayed for password text fields

### Code sample

<details><summary>Code sample</summary>

This is the widget I’m using in Flutter:
```
class MaterialTextFieldView extends StatelessWidget {
  const MaterialTextFieldView({super.key});

  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return Scaffold(
      appBar: AppBar(
        backgroundColor: Theme.of(context).colorScheme.inversePrimary,
        title: const Text('Text fields'),
      ),
      body: const SingleChildScrollView(
        padding: EdgeInsets.symmetric(
          horizontal: 24,
          vertical: 16,
        ),
        child: Column(
          mainAxisAlignment: MainAxisAlignment.center,
          children: <Widget>[
            TextField(
              decoration: InputDecoration(
                labelText: 'Left to right',
              ),
            ),
            SizedBox(height: 48),
            TextField(
              decoration: InputDecoration(
                labelText: 'Right to left',
              ),
              textDirection: TextDirection.rtl,

            SizedBox(height: 48),

              maxLines: 2,
              decoration: InputDecoration(labelText: 'Max lines (2)'),

            ),
            SizedBox(height: 48),
            PasswordTextField(),
            SizedBox(height: 48),
            PhoneNumberTextField(),
            SizedBox(height: 48),
          ],
        ),
      ),
    );
  }
}
```

The `triaged-text-input` label is irrelevant if there is no `team-text-input` label or `fyi-text-input` label.

It looked interesting, so I checked it out and it appeared that this behaviour could not be changed.

---

In Safari, software keyboard does not switch to trackpad mode when `type="password"` is set. This behavior can be cheked by opening the MDN page on the iPhone. The username field can be switched to trackpad mode, but not the password field.

https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Element/input/password

So, to resolve this issue, it is necessary to always set `element.type = 'text';` in the following places.

https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/3.29.0/engine/src/flutter/lib/web_ui/lib/src/engine/text_editing/text_editing.dart#L510-L518

However, PasswordManager cannot autofill `new-password` or `current-password` into an input of type `text`. Therefore, this difference in behaviour between Safari and iOS native apps is strange but seems impossible to fix.

The suggested solution only works for selecting by dragging mouse, but not by moving handles. So there should probably be more profound changes to make scrolling selection works with handles as well.

`TextSelectionOverlay._handleSelectionHandleChanged` calls `selectionDelegate.bringIntoView(textPosition)`, which would be enough, but there are two more similar calls that messed things up for handles. These are `EditableTextState._scheduleShowCaretOnScreen` and `selectionDelegate.bringIntoView(textPosition)` in `_TextFieldState._handleSelectionChanged`. And that is only for a single selection change.

One solution would be to add `handle` value to `SelectionChangedCause` enum and make `TextSelectionOverlay._handleSelectionHandleChanged` specify it when calling `TextSelectionDelegate.userUpdateTextEditingValue`, which can then avoid bringing selection end into view assuming that a caller knows better what point should be revealed. Another option would be to extend `TextSelectionDelegate.userUpdateTextEditingValue`'s interface by adding one extra parameter explicitly telling whether it should reveal an update.

As for `_TextFieldState._handleSelectionChanged`, it seems that a part where `bringIntoView` called can be simply removed.

Thanks for the code sample @Oleksa5, this issue looks the same as https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/102484, Where you have two scrollable (ListView, TextField with dynamic height) However, I am labeling this issue as a proposal to implement the above changes. I will leave this for the team to decide.

cc: @justinmc
I agree that the root problem is probably the same.  I'll take another look at a solution in https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/102992.

After investigating, I think my solution in https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/102992 of using Scrollable.of to compensate for the outer scrollable isn't enough.  It will still be blocked by the problem detailed in this issue.  If I try it without addressing this issue, I get this kind of bounce:

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/389558/170798802-809e40f3-3d81-4f21-bb11-a228e1dedcf9.mov

Interestingly, I didn't see that bounce until I merged in master.  So commit https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/102992/commits/bd37072dfc8bf090bd7420c60ecf5cc3a474c544 didn't bounce and worked fine without considering this issue.  Something must have changed in the last ~3 weeks.

Either way now, I think my PR will need to address both issues.
https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/102992 will merge shortly, and with my testing it seems to fix this issue on desktop but not on mobile.
Didn't mean to close this, we should figure out what the problem is on mobile.
This issue is missing a priority label. Please set a priority label when adding the `triaged-design` label.

issues.title: Directional navigation key binding defaults should be limited to those platforms that use it.
issues.body:
<!-- Thank you for using Flutter!

     If you are looking for support, please check out our documentation
     or consider asking a question on Stack Overflow:
      * https://flutter.dev/
      * https://api.flutter.dev/
      * https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/flutter?sort=frequent

     If you have found a bug or if our documentation doesn't have an answer
     to what you're looking for, then fill our the template below. Please read
     our guide to filing a bug first: https://flutter.dev/docs/resources/bug-reports
-->

When running the fluter devtools on macos, I'm seeing strange key handling behavior. There are extra key actions that I don't see on flutter web. Some discussion of the issue, with screenshots, is at https://github.com/flutter/devtools/issues/1769. But essentially:

I'm adding row selection and keyboard navigation to a custom widget TreeTable ([built here](https://github.com/flutter/devtools/blob/ae483b098ec65941f457bf545bdc9059f6537231/packages/devtools_app/lib/src/flutter/table.dart#L363)). I'm explicitly [handling the arrow up/down keys](https://github.com/flutter/devtools/blob/ae483b098ec65941f457bf545bdc9059f6537231/packages/devtools_app/lib/src/flutter/table.dart#L427) to move the selection and scroll the view. But on macos I see scrolling that is not caused by my handler, and also a kind of ghost selected row (see the screenshots in https://github.com/flutter/devtools/issues/1769) not caused by me (the color is different than the one I am using). Also, sometimes after clicking on a row the arrow keys will start cycling through the top-level tabs (Inspector, timeline) instead of the rows in the table.

In a nutshell there is some code handling the up/down arrow keys in a way that I don't understand, can't control, and can't predict. It also does not happen when I run via flutter web which leads me to believe it's a bug in the macos platform code.

The PR that introduced keyboard navigation is here: https://github.com/flutter/devtools/pull/1810

## Steps to Reproduce
$git clone git@github.com:flutter/devtools.git
$cd devtools/packages/devtools_app
$flutter run -d macos

1. Enter the url of another running flutter app
2. Click on the "info" tab
3. Click "performance"
4. Record performance data
5. Click on the "call tree" tab
6. Click inside the call tree table
7. navigate up/down with the arrow keys.
8. Compare with the behavior when running with `-d web`

**Expected results:** <!-- what did you want to see? -->
I expect the arrow up/down keys to cause the window to scroll only when the selection reaches the bottom or top of the viewport. I expect there to be only 1 selected row. I expect that pressing the arrow up/down keys should not escape the treetable and start cycling the top-level tabs of the viewport.

**Actual results:** <!-- what did you see? -->
See the examples at https://github.com/flutter/devtools/issues/1769

  <summary>Logs</summary>

      log output below between the lines with the backticks. If there is an

-->


```

comments:
I just noticed that a similar thing happens in the "flutter inspector" tab. This is without any specific key handling for the arrow keys. Focus travels through the inspector tree and other UI elements in an unpredictable way.

In this example I just mouse-click on the root element and then press the up/down/left/right arrow keys to see what happens.

![inspector-arrow-keys](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3227/79902998-363e3b00-83c7-11ea-815d-2a7d90db21c2.gif)
cc @stuartmorgan

I noticed that I can get similar behavior from the flutter gallery app.

In macos, the arrow keys move through the buttons:
![gallery-arrow-keys](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3227/79906160-2d039d00-83cc-11ea-8b0e-4968da65c1f7.gif)

Similar to what "tab" does in flutter web; but while "tab" cycles through the UI elements, the macos arrow keys are "smart": left/right moves strictly back and forth and up/down moves focus vertically (example: with the "info" button highlighted, pressing down will focus "sign in").

In simple cases I could see this as desirable behavior, but with the kind of custom UI we have in the devtools it definitely is not. Maybe I just need to know how to turn it off?

> Maybe I just need to know how to turn it off?

I believe you can turn it off per https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/45102, but absent a strong argument in favor of having them, I think the right fix here is to have these not be part of the default set on desktop.

You're right, I think these are probably not correct for macOS. I'll disable them.

I did look into the user interface guidelines for keyboard traversal on macOS at the time I defined those, but I didn't find guidance that was all that helpful there one way or another.

When you disable them for macOS, maybe refactor a bit to have _defaultDesktopShortcuts, so that we don't have the same bug crop up for Linux and Windows? We can always re-specialize them later if we find places we want to diverge.
Linux (Debian) at least (I can't test windows at the moment) does do this kind of navigation, at least in their system settings app and a couple of others I tested.
I'm seeing some control-specific things on Windows (e.g., if I'm in a list, or in a menu, then it navigates within that context, as on macOS), but nothing that jumps to other control areas.

Actually, just disabling it won't work.  I'm going to need to disable it at the top level, and then add it back in for each of the things that should be able to navigate in that way (e.g. for a list view, you should be able to navigate in the scroll axis).

@gspencergoog Ah. This only partially helps me, since both my widgets are rendered in ListViews.  Is there a way to control key navigation at that level? https://github.com/flutter/devtools/blob/6d9cd23f61c3bd98a32d84acbce0d25968b49021/packages/devtools_app/lib/src/flutter/table.dart#L661

You can always just turn it off, or bind it to another action.

To turn it off, you can put a `Shortcuts` widget around your view with those keys bound to "Intent.doNothing".  Or bind them to your intent (and action) if you want to do something different.

Or, if you want to turn off directional navigation, but don't want to know the keys it is bound to, you can also bind a `DirectionalFocusIntent` to `DoNothingAction()` in an `Actions` widget around your view.
Is this still valid?
Yes, this is still valid.
The `triaged-desktop` label is irrelevant if there is no `team-desktop` label or `fyi-desktop` label.

issues.title: [iOS] Autocorrect highlight does not go away if you scroll
issues.body:

In a text field, type a misspelled word like `grept`. Don't accept the autocorrect suggestion. > 2. Do a swipe gesture to scroll. > * **Expected behavior**: Scrolling accepts the autocorrect suggestion. The autocorrect highlight is no longer visible. > * **Actual behavior**: Scrolling accepts the autocorrect suggestion, but the autocorrect highlight remains visible at the text field's original location, which is clearly incorrect as the text field scrolls away. > The expected behavior is what `UITextField` and `UITextView` do right? IIRC `UIScrollView` sends out a `NSNotification` (with a private notification name) on scroll and the autocorrect dismisses when it receives that notification.

import 'package:flutter/material.dart';

    return MaterialApp(

    );
  }

## Steps to Reproduce

<!-- Please include full steps to reproduce so that we can reproduce the problem. -->

1. Paste the code sample below into dartpad.dev
2. Type some text in the text field
3. Hit enter/return on your keyboard
4. Observe that the print statement is logged twice
5. Follow the same steps on MacOS (or any other non-web platform) and observe that the print statement is logged once

**Expected results:** <!-- what did you want to see? -->
`onFieldSubmitted` is submitted once.

**Actual results:** <!-- what did you see? -->
Calling `TextInput.finishAutofillContext()` causes `onFieldSubmitted` to be submitted a second time.


<!--
      Please create a minimal reproducible sample that shows the problem
      and attach it below between the lines with the backticks.

      To create it you can use `flutter create bug` command and update the `main.dart` file.

      which is capable of creating and running small Flutter apps.

      we regretfully will have to close it.

```dart
import 'package:flutter/material.dart';
import 'package:flutter/services.dart';

void main() => runApp(const MyApp());

class MyApp extends StatelessWidget {
  const MyApp({super.key});

  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return  MaterialApp(
      home: Scaffold(
        body: Padding(
          padding: const EdgeInsets.all(8.0),
          child: TextFormField(
            onFieldSubmitted: (value) {
              print('onFieldSubmitted');
              TextInput.finishAutofillContext(shouldSave: false);
            },
          ),
        ),
      ),
    );
  }
}
```

issues.title: Usage of form validators doesn't feel right - Proposal for better return type
comments:
Hi @Mereep 
I do think the current API reasy to understand: return a error message or return null to indicate that there is not error to show. From what I can see the approach you are suggesting does not get rid of the null value it just makes it implicit.

This would add just too much complexity into the code base.

Can you elaborate more what is exactly wrong with the current API and why the one you suggested is better?

Cc @justinmc

This is a really interesting issue, thanks for starting this discussion.  I admit that I was thrown off by validator returning null to mean success as well when I first learned it.  These are my initial thoughts:

  * Changing validator to have a return type of dynamic would probably do more harm than good because it would break static analysis of the return type. (I admit this would be much improved if https://github.com/dart-lang/language/issues/83 was fixed.)
  * The Result pattern is nice (I'm also a fan of Rust), but it would be an anomaly here when it's not used anywhere else in Flutter.  This idea might be too big for a small change like this.  When do we use error throwing and when do we use Result? Maybe I'm thinking too big though.
  * Simply renaming `validator` to something like `getValidationErrorMessage` might solve a lot of this kind of confusion as well.

In the meantime your Result class might make a nice pub package if you're inclined and others want to use this pattern.

Hi @justinmc,


Thanks for your thoughts on that. I will try to share mine.

Yes you're right this change is not as small as it seems. But not as big either. Right, using `dynamic` or `object` for that matter will break the analysis as long as it neither is replaced by the actual return type (breaking change) or https://github.com/dart-lang/language/issues/83 is fixed. However, The static **analyser doesn't help right now either**. The static analyser is not hinting the user to return `null` for success in the first place. In fact it doesn't even know that `return null` is valid at all.  

Also. how to implement a situation where the success case can also carry information? Throw? Throw sometimes? Invent a new type? `Result` will also fix those scenarios (think of some `HttpResponse` for example)

> When do we use error throwing and when do we use Result?

That is a very important point:

- Especially from the user perspective. Where should the user know that a validation error is not to be reported by throwing a `ValidationError`? This would even be a better interface right now in my opinion.  Its just not possible without digging the whole library. You can only hint on implicit conventions which is almost never a good idea. Having the `Result` return type makes that explicit and crystal clear while giving full static analyzer help.

- From the framework developer perspective I would first introduce it to any kind of callbacks the user is to implement in order to not have him or her decide that tricky question their own. Also, I would introduce the **`Result`** type anywhere I suspect something to **naturally fail or succeed** which is **not** due to a **unexpected** condition. A validation function is a perfect example. But also a network request fits into that scheme. Further, functions like `obtainUserCoordinates` which are very likely to fail for different reasons (no permission, no connection, ...) or succeed (also with additional information like the type of the reporting provider)

>  it would be an anomaly here when it's not used anywhere else in Flutter.

Honestly, I don't know where Flutter uses comparable scenarios. I don't have too much flutter experience just yet (in fact I'm writing my first App using flutter atm.).  That said, the pattern is very clear, doesn't put additional cognitive load to the user and can be introduced in new APIs while slowly deprecating the old ones.

>  renaming `validator` to something like `getValidationErrorMessage`

This would be a breaking change itself and also feels like fixed with duct tape somehow ;-)

Seeing all the work that is put on explicit (non-)nullability in Dart it seems obvious to me that having `null` returns isn't  the best  idea in the first place. 

To conclude, I think it is worthy considering a slow migration due to the non-breaking properties, the clear consumer code, clear APIs, long-run API consistency and increased confidence when consuming APIs. An option would also be to introduce the change to new comparable APIs while waiting for https://github.com/dart-lang/language/issues/83 to be introduced to the language to port the old ones.

Thanks for the well thought out response.  I think I agree with you for the most part, but the next question is: what can we actually do about this right now?

Flutter has an issue (https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/24722) for collecting big breaking changes that we may want to consider for large version changes.  You might want to add this idea and link to this issue if you think it's appropriate.

You also might want to write a [design doc](https://flutter.dev/go/template) explaining the breaking change and the reasoning in this issue.  I think if you find a small-ish reasonable change that we can make now and try to argue for it, we might be able to start using this pattern in that one small place.

issues.title: EditableText does not call onEditingComplete on focus loss (macOS)
issues.body:
On desktop operating systems, it's typical for certain types of focus loss (via mouse input or keyboard navigation) to commit a text field's value, but Flutter's `EditableText` widget does not invoke the equivalent lifecycle method, `onEditingComplete`, in this situation. It appears that the only way to invoke the method on desktop is through an enter press.

I believe this to be in error, as the mobile equivalent to a tab/shift+tab press (`TextInputAction.next`, `TextInputAction.previous`) invokes `onEditingComplete`.

cc @gspencergoog
comments:
I was able to reproduce the latest `master` channel. The code bellow should print `onEditingComplete() called` when the first field is focused and TAB is pressed to navigate to the second text field.

<details><summary>sample code</summary>

```
import 'package:flutter/material.dart';

void main() {
  runApp(MyApp());
}

class MyApp extends StatelessWidget {
  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return MaterialApp(
      title: 'Demo',
      home: Scaffold(
        body: Center(
          child: Column(
            mainAxisSize: MainAxisSize.min,
            children: [
              EditableText(
                focusNode: FocusNode(),
                controller: TextEditingController(text: '(field 1) click me'),
                backgroundCursorColor: Colors.blue,
                cursorColor: Colors.red,
                style: TextStyle(color: Colors.grey),
                onEditingComplete: () {
                  print('onEditingComplete() called');
                },
              ),
              EditableText(
                focusNode: FocusNode(),
                controller: TextEditingController(text: 'field 2'),
                backgroundCursorColor: Colors.blue,
                cursorColor: Colors.red,
                style: TextStyle(color: Colors.black),
                onEditingComplete: () {

                },
              ),
            ],
          ),
        ),
      ),
    );
  }
}

Also happens on web with widgets like `TextField` and `TextFormField` 

https://stackoverflow.com/questions/65634007/oneditingcomplete-is-not-called-after-unfocus lists a solution which is to wrap the   offending widget in a `Focus` widget which may well work but shouldn't (IMHO!) be needed.
The `triaged-desktop` label is irrelevant if there is no `team-desktop` label or `fyi-desktop` label.

This issue also annoys me on **Linux**.
But I found a [rather old comment](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/49785#issuecomment-649902991) in a complementary issue that was fixed. It goes like this:
> (1) Both onEditingComplete and onSubmitted are called at the same time.
 (2) onSubmitted (which has the value) is indicates user the text editing is complete.
 (3) onEditingComplete is for overriding the default behavior of focus changes.

So, apparently, if someone needs a _coarser_ update than `onChanged()` on the `TextField`s content, they need to also intercept the losing of focus, beside setting `onSubmit()`. See this [StackOverflow answer](https://stackoverflow.com/a/65767705) as a starting point.

Thanks for the repro! The problem appears to be `TextInput.finishAutofillContext` is getting called several frames late (because of the route transition animation) during `unmount` after the autofocused new text field is already on screen and has focus. 

Unfortunately I think telling the os to remove only specific text fields doesn't work, as on Android there is no such api to only commit a subset of text fields that's in the current `Activity`, so pretty much the only viable option will be to fix the timing of the `finishAutofillContext` call. I'm thinking maybe each `AutofillGroup` should by default hook into it's route's `willPop` callback and call `TextInput.finishAutofillContext` there instead? /cc @chunhtai

is it because of TextInput can only attach to one client at a time? if so I think TextInput.finishAutofillContext should be smart enough to handle calls when the caller is not the current client.

The other way of fixing this without touching too much of the API will be listening to https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/Route/isCurrent.html or https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/Route/isActive.html
in deChangeDependency. Not sure if that will make it before the new text field in on the new route received focus

The limitation comes from the Android `commit` autofill context API where **everything** in the current `Activity` will be committed or discarded (and Flutter currently has one `Activity` afaik). I'll give `isCurrent` a try, thanks for the pointers!

issues.title: Inconsistent behavior of RawKeyboardListener on different devices/Android versions
issues.body:
I am trying to get my flutter app to react to events generated when media keys are pressed on a little bluetooth keyboard paired with my android device.

Here's the code snippet that builds the widget I use to evaluate the scenario.

![raw-keyboard-listener-code](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1816194/71897913-dc836f00-314f-11ea-8bc9-30f23e1ec891.png)

If I test it on my Google Pixel C tablet running under Android 8.1.0, kernel version 3.18.0-g8ce8444 it works as expected, When the widget is displayed I can press the media buttons on the keyboard and the events are properly generated and passed to the app, handlers are fired etc.

On the other hand, if I test exactly the same code on my Samsung S9 running under Android 9, kernel version 4.9.59-17174122 my attempts to press the media buttons on the keyboard will take no effect until I press one of the numeric keys. After that, the following lines appear in the log 

![Screenshot from 2020-01-07 13:28:00](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1816194/71898579-9c24f080-3151-11ea-988c-ac8e52178627.png)

After that the media keys on the keyboard work as expected.
Is there any workaround to that problem?

Finally a question regarding iOS. I am aware that RawKeyboardListerer is not implemented for iOS, but is there a way for a flutter app running on iOS device to react to media buttons like in the above scenario?

cc from text-input triage @dkwingsmt @gspencergoog did you have any thoughts on this issue? I know there is `KeyboardListener` available now, is that preferred over RawKeyboardListener?

Yes, `KeyboardListener` is preferred over `RawKeyboardListener`, but I'm not sure that will have any effect on this problem.

This seems like the cause is something lower down in the stack, since the different Android distributions produce different results. It could be that Samsung just doesn't produce the same key events at a low level.  To debug this, we'd need to get the devices and see what it is producing when those keys are pressed.

It's interesting that the behavior changes when you focus a text field. The text field does open up an [InputConnection](https://developer.android.com/reference/android/view/inputmethod/InputConnection) when focused, so maybe that is what is turning it on on Samsung, but it's always on for Pixel?

That particular keyboard seems to be relying on undocumented keyboard handling behavior implemented in a private class, in this method `-[UITextInputController _insertText:fromKeyboard:]`. Specifically, in `__50-[UITextInputController _insertText:fromKeyboard:]_block_invoke.591`,  selected text is temporarily changed so text `ああ` can be inserted to the very start of the user input string, while the "actual" caret is after the current composing text.

I wasn't able to figure out the exact logic `UITextField` uses to adjust the string or why it does that. I suspect it's a hack. We can do something similar to this, like when there's composing text, `-[UITextInput insertText:]` always insert the text before the composing text, but I'm a bit worried that adding this hack may break some other text interactions.
This issue is missing a priority label. Please set a priority label when adding the `triaged-design` label.

issues.title: TextField RTL input problem with LTR letters/numbers while obscureText is true
issues.body:
I believe there's a bug with textfield when writing LTR characters in an obscured field.

The characters are not written in the correct direction while typing (this issue appears only when obscureText is true).

The input value actually is submitted correctly with no problems and also if the password is made visible it is shown in the right way with no issues, the issue only is while typing so it makes it feel like if the password is written in a reversed direction.

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Create an empty application.
2. Add a TextField  or TextFormField to the application.
3. Set textDirection: TextDirection.rtl
4. Set obscureText: true.
5. type something in English.


![screen-20201127-184354](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/30502213/100470646-51052a80-30e1-11eb-84a7-838968ba2232.gif)

To act the same like when obscureText: false.

issues.title: TextField cursor display when the keyboard is closed should match the Platform
issues.body:
In native development:

* In the **Android**, the text-field cursor should keep blinking when the soft keyboard is closed and the text-field has focus.
* In the **iPhone**, the text-field cursor should hide as soon as the keyboard is closed.

Flutter is only implementing the Android behavior. I'd like the `TextField` to implement this difference automatically, to match the Platform.

Please note: I know we can make `TextField(showCursor: false...)` to hide the cursor, but I can´t achieve that myself since I don´t know for sure when the keyboard is closed/open, and also some devices have physical keyboards.
comments: cc: @justinmc

@maheshmnj in your native iOS video, you dismiss the keyboard by pressing "return", but on Android, you dismiss it by pressing that "down triangle button".  Maybe these two operations are not equivalent.  On native iOS (simulator), if I dismiss the keyboard using cmd + K, then the cursor continues blinking:

| native Android | native iOS |
| -- | -- |
| <video src="https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/389558/136835014-5b821e8f-ea32-4b44-80d9-09edd98e11a1.mov" /> | <video src="https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/389558/136835001-a010cefd-67c3-4248-b376-79c9b1b94c3e.mov" /> |

Maybe this is working as intended?  Or if not, we need to clarify exactly what sorts of operations should remove the cursor.

Here is how we could naively remove the cursor on keyboard hide on iOS, but this doesn't seem to match native exactly: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/compare/master...justinmc:cursor-blink-ios?expand=1

> @maheshmnj in your native iOS video, you dismiss the keyboard by pressing "return", but on Android

Sorry about that, looks like even on Native ios hiding keyboard doesn't remove cursor/ focus of textfield.Probably this is working as intended.

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/31410839/136904657-87c5483b-47f2-4c1c-95f1-ec6c71fef4d2.mov

_hiding keyboard (using cmd  + k) doesn't remove focus from input field_

> Or if not, we need to clarify exactly what sorts of operations should remove the cursor.

@marcglasberg  Can you please clarify this?
 As mentioned above even in the native IOS app the cursor remains after hiding the keyboard.

@justinmc @maheshmnj In the iPhone we dismiss the keyboard by tapping some non-clickable area of the screen (or sometimes by swiping down clickable or scrollable areas). There is no keyboard in an iPhone. If the Emulator lets us keep the cursor by pressing `CMD + K`, that's not relevant for end users. End users don't use the emulator, they use a real phone.

I have tested those apps, with a real iPhone, by tapping (or sometimes swiping down):

* Reminder (iOS native) ➜ It hides the cursor
* Messages Search bar (iOS native) ➜ It hides the cursor
* WhatsApp ➜ It hides the cursor
* Google Chrome Search Bar ➜ It hides the cursor
* YouTube Search Bar ➜ It hides the cursor

As you can see, it always hides the cursor. Let me know if you need me to test other apps. Or, if you have a real iPhone, please try it yourself.

I was asked to fix this by a client. I actually like the cursor blinking there, but that's not the native behavior for the iPhone, and people notice it. 

Thank you.

@justinmc 

Please try WhatsApp in iOS, and Telegram in iOS. Go to a conversation and then:

If you scroll the conversation, the keyboard remains open and the textfield remains focused.
If you tap the conversation (without scrolling), the keyboard closes and the textfield is unfocused.
But these areas are not really non-clickable really, since they can be scrolled. It seems to be rare to find non-scrollable areas in iOS apps.

However, in Pinterest, during the login, when you are typing your email or password, if you click a non-clickable area below, and it closes the keyboard and the textfield is unfocused.

If you can make it configurable, I think that's probably the best decision.
Any solution for this?

issues.body:
### Steps to reproduce

### Without my repository
1. Focus on TextField
2. Unfocus it
3. Open bottom sheet
4. Focus on TextField inside this bottom sheet

Steps from 2 to 4 should be done very quick.  
You can open bottom sheet with button, which unfocuses first textfield as well. And have the second TextField inside bottom sheet to have autofocus.  
Then, this steps will be done in one action.    


### With my repository
1. Clone the repository from link below
2. Run app.
3. Tap on TextField on top to focus on it
4. Tap on button on bottom

It may take more than one attempt to reproduce.  
It seams it depends on some sort of 'lag'. You can increase image count in app to create this lag. This increases the chance to trigger the issue.

Bottom sheet opens up. Keyboard opens. Textfield inside bottom sheet is in focus.

Yeah, so I checked this out, and it’s probably some weird timing issue with how Flutter handles focus changes. Like, the first TextField loses focus, but then when the bottom sheet opens, the keyboard kinda resets and closes itself even though the new TextField actually gets focus.

Couple of things you could try:

Instead of **autofocus: true**, try delaying the focus request a bit with **Future.delayed(Duration(milliseconds: 200)).**

Make sure the first TextField properly unfocuses before opening the bottom sheet **(FocusScope.of(context).unfocus())**, so there’s no weird overlap.

You can also try wrapping the focus request inside **WidgetsBinding.instance.addPostFrameCallback**, so it only happens after everything is built.

@AHAHACUK 
Check [this](https://docs.flutter.dev/ui/interactivity/focus) document and see if it helps to properly implement your code and further helps in resolving the reported behavior or not.

The problem is only with keyboard. It hides for no reason, even if TextField is focused correctly.

This behaviour occurs only on Android it seems. If it helps.  
On IOS keyboard is shown as expected.

@darshankawar  
Yes, `autofocus: false` works as expected. But if you are suggesting this as a fix, then it is not suitable, since TextField won't be focused when bottom sheet opens up, which is requested app behaviour.  

Also, I don't think this issue is in 'autofocus' property. Since you can disable autofocus, and requestFocus on TextField manually. This will create the same issue as described above.  

I don't know is this issue occurs in native Android. If you are suggesting that it may be non-Flutter platform issue, then yeah, I suppose it is possible.

There have been similar reports earlier:

https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/125565

issues.title: iOS: NSJSONSerialization crash when deleting SMP characters
issues.body:
**Describe the bug**
When deleting Supplementary Multilingual Plane (SMP) characters on iOS, the text input system leaves orphaned UTF-16 surrogates in the NSString. This causes NSJSONSerialization to fail with "failed to convert to UTF8" error and crash the app.

**Steps to reproduce**
1. Create a Flutter app with a TextField or text input widget
2. On an iOS device, paste this string: **ᴿ᭄𝑹ahul Rajput**
3. Delete it character by character until only 1-2 characters remain
4. App crashes with: "Invalid JSON message, encoding failed: (null)"

**Expected behavior**
Text input should handle surrogate pairs correctly without crashing

**Actual behavior**
NSJSONSerialization rejects the string because it contains lone (unpaired) surrogates from incomplete SMP character encoding

**Proposed Solution**
Before JSON serialization, validate and sanitize UTF-16 surrogates:
- Drop lone/orphaned surrogates
- Keep valid surrogate pairs
- Preserve all BMP characters
- Remap text-editing cursor indices

**Code Fix**
The fix has been implemented in the Engine layer (FlutterCodecs.mm):
- Added `sanitizeJSONObject()` function to recursively clean JSON object trees
- Added helper functions for surrogate detection and index remapping
- Special handling for text-editing state dicts

Commit: `5ffdf6f689e18fe0f6b3045f0b07453d4a0c3266`
Branch: `fix/ios-surrogate-utf16-crash`

**Platform**
- iOS 12.0+

**Related Issues**
GitHub Issue #142327

issues.title: Rebuild Autocomplete options at arbitrary time
issues.body:
### Use case

When the user finishes typing in the field, a user of Autocomplete might make two network requests: one to quickly fetch some approximate options and another, longer request to get more detailed results. Autocomplete should be able to update the options with the result of each request. Currently the optionsViewBuilder is only called in response to the completion of the future returned by optionsBuilder.

### Proposal

The hacky solution is probably to introduce a controller with a method to imperatively update the options: `AutocompleteController.setOptions`.

A better solution might require a breaking change. Really it seems like maybe the options should just be passed into Autocomplete as a parameter? That would also require the ability to listen to changes in the field.

### Related

There are other request to be able to display things in the options view at certain times, for example while loading: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/147377. Solving this issue would probably also fix that.

issues.title: When TextField enters Chinese in Windows 10, the candidate list will flicker
issues.body:
### Is there an existing issue for this?

- [X] I have searched the [existing issues](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues)
- [X] I have read the [guide to filing a bug](https://flutter.dev/docs/resources/bug-reports)

### Steps to reproduce

It is found that when running the Flutter program on Windows 10, when entering Chinese into the TextField component, there will always be flickering shadows in the candidate list of the input method. I have tried other input methods and the same problem occurs.

### Expected results


Under normal circumstances, such flickering shadows should not appear.

### Actual results

Under normal circumstances, there should be no afterimage flickering in the input method candidate list

### Code sample

<details><summary>Code sample</summary>

```dart
import 'package:flutter/material.dart';

main() {
  runApp(const MaterialApp(
    home: MainPage(),
  ));
}

class MainPage extends StatelessWidget {
  const MainPage({Key? key}) : super(key: key);

  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return Scaffold(
      appBar: AppBar(
        title: const Text('AppBar'),
      ),
      body: Container(
        child:TextField(
          minLines: 10,
          maxLines: 20,
        ),
      ),
    );
  }
}

```

(Desktop Triage) We think that this is related to repositioning asynchronously where sometimes we lose the race, placing the suggestions at 0,0 for a frame. We think that the correct behavior should be that the pop up candidate list shouldn't move along with the cursor, and only reposition it when the user starts typing again. If what you're typing moves down to a new line, then we should also reposition. We would then update a lot less often, making the times we lose the race much less likely.

That approach sounds good to me (looking at this in text input triage with @Renzo-Olivares @LongCatIsLooong).  Trying it on native Mac, the menu does reposition itself after each commit, but it probably won't be very noticeable if it doesn't on Flutter.  Definitely better than this flashing.
